elements.sent-2411-001: Radiation is a universal friction-increasing agent.
elements.sent-2411-002: When two fluid layers are in relative motion, the inevitable exchange of radiation between such layers gives rise to an effective force, which tries to prevent the layers from sliding.
elements.sent-2411-003: This friction is often modeled as a Navier-Stokes shear viscosity.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, non-Newtonian corrections are expected to appear at distances of about one optical depth from the layers' interface.
elements.sent-2411-005: Such corrections prevent the viscous stress from becoming too large.
elements.sent-2411-006: Here, we set the foundations of a rigorous theory for these corrections, valid along incompressible flows.
elements.sent-2411-007: We show that, in the linear regime, the infinite Chapman-Enskog series can be computed analytically, leading to universal formulas for all transport coefficients, which apply to any fluid, with any composition, with radiation of any type (also neutrinos), and with nearly any type of radiative process.
elements.sent-2411-008: We then show that, with an appropriate shear-heat coupling coefficient, Israel-Stewart theory can correctly describe most non-Newtonian features of radiative shear stresses.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.44\linewidth]{Friction.PNG} \caption{Microscopic origin of radiative shear viscosity. Consider a fluid that moves along the $x^3$ axis, with some velocity $v>0$ in the region $R^+=\{x^1{>}0\}$, and opposite velocity $-v$ in $R^-=\{x^1{<}0\}$. On average, radiation particles (yellow) emitted in $R^-$ carry negative momentum in direction $x^3$, since their source is negatively boosted. Hence, if they are reabsorbed inside $R^+$, they will impress (on average) a negative kick on the fluid. Similarly, radiation emitted from $R^+$ impresses a positive kick when absorbed in $R^-$. The result is a frictional force between the fluid layers, a.k.a. a shear viscosity. } \label{fig:friction}
elements.sent-2411-009: Introduction - Our ability to model high-mass stars, optically thick accretion disks, supernovae, and neutron-star mergers heavily depends on our understanding of how matter and thermal radiation (photons or neutrinos) interact at macroscopic (i.e. fluid-dynamic) scales, see e.g. \cite{Bruenn1985,Rampp2002,Rampp:2002bq,Farris2008,Chiavassa2011,Fragile:2014bfa,Mirizzi:2015eza,Radice:2016dwd,Murchikova:2017zsy,Fragile:2018xee,Lancova:2019ldt,Perego:2019adq,Anninos2020,Fukushima2021,Foucart:2022bth,RadiceNew2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: This problem is so important for astrophysical modeling that there is a dedicated branch of fluid mechanics, called ``radiation hydrodynamics'', whose central goal is to develop effective descriptions of the radiation field, which may be implemented in numerical simulations \cite{Pomraning1973,mihalas_book,CastorRadiationBook_2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Most aspects of the radiation-hydrodynamic problem (e.g. radiative heat conduction and bulk viscosity \cite{NovikovThorne1973,weinbergQFT_1995,UdeyIsrael1982,Minerbo1978,Levermore1984,GavassinoRadiazione ) are at this point well-understood, and current numerical schemes, especially the M1 closure \cite{Minerbo1978,Levermore1984,ShibataRadiation2011k,Sadowski2013,RadiceNew2022 , capture the essential physics quite reliably.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, to date, radiation hydrodynamics has failed to provide a systematic and rigorous theory for radiative shear viscosity, whose foundations we aim to set here.
elements.sent-2411-013: State of the art - Let us first review what is currently known.
elements.sent-2411-014: The microscopic picture is very simple \cite[\S 97]{mihalas_book : Two fluid layers in relative motion exchange radiation, and in doing so redistribute their momentum, thereby reducing their relative speed, see figure \ref{fig:friction}\footnote{Throughout the article, we work in Minkowski spacetime, with metric signature $(-,+,+,+)$, and we adopt natural units, such that $c=\hbar=k_B=1$.}}

elements.section-2411-001: Universal behavior of the radiative transport equation for transversal flows
elements.sent-2411-122: The Boltzmann equation for an ideal massless gas mixture interacting with a medium can be decomposed as \begin{equation}\label{sgrem} p^\mu \partial_\mu f^A_{p} = \sum_{\text{``processes''}} [\mathcal{C}^A_{p}]_\text{``process''}\, ,\end{equation} where each term $[\mathcal{C ^A_{p ]_\text{``process''}$ on the right-hand side is a collision integral associated with a certain interaction process.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: If we linearize this equation for a plane-wave perturbation $\delta \Psi(t,x^1)$ away from equilibrium, we obtain \begin{equation}\label{whatIwant} \bigg(\partial_t {+}\dfrac{p^1}{p^0} \partial_1 \bigg) \delta f^A_{p}= \sum_{\text{``processes''}} \dfrac{1}{p^0} \, \delta [\mathcal{C}^A_{p}]_\text{``process''}\end{equation} In this section, we will show that, along transversal flows, the most common interaction processes are such that \begin{equation}\label{griecz} \dfrac{1}{p^0} \, \delta [\mathcal{C}^A_{p}]_\text{``process''} =[\mathcal{S}^A(p^0)]_{\text{``process''}} \, p^3 \delta u^3 -\sum_B[\mathcal{M}\indices{^A _B}(p^0)]_{\text{``process''}} \, \delta f^B_{p} \, ,\end{equation} for some related coefficients $[\mathcal{S ^A(p^0)]_{\text{``process'' }$ and $[\mathcal{M}\indices{^A _B (p^0)]_{\text{``process'' }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-124: The relevant formulas for the interaction processes below are taken from \cite{Bruenn1985,Rampp2002,Levermore1984,ShibataRadiation2011k,Richers:2019grc .}
elements.sent-2411-125: In the fluid's local rest frame, the collision integral due to absorption and emission of radiation by the fluid reads \begin{equation}\label{absorp} [\mathcal{C}^A_{p}]_\text{``abs''} =p^0\bigg(\kappa_A(p^0)-\sigma_{A,a}(p^0)f^A_{p} \bigg) \, ,\end{equation} where $\kappa_A$ and $\sigma_{A,a}$ are the emission and absorption coefficients (already corrected with final-state terms).
elements.sent-2411-126: Since the collision integral and the distribution function are Lorentz scalars, equation \eqref{absorp corresponds to the following covariant expression, which is true in all reference frames: \begin{equation}\label{absorp2} [\mathcal{C}^A_{p}]_\text{``abs''} =-u_\mu p^\mu\bigg(\kappa_A(-u_\mu p^\mu)-\sigma_{A,a}(-u_\mu p^\mu)f^A_{p} \bigg) \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-127: Linearizing this expression for a transversal flow (as discussed in the main text), we obtain \begin{equation}\label{sgrom} \begin{split} \delta [\mathcal{C}^A_{p}]_\text{``abs''} ={} -p^3 \delta u^3 \bigg(\kappa_A(p^0)-\sigma_{A,a}(p^0)f^A_{p} \bigg) - p^0 p^3 \delta u^3 \bigg(\dfrac{d\kappa_A(p^0)}{dp^0}-\dfrac{d \sigma_{A,a}(p^0)}{dp^0}f^A_{p} \bigg) -p^0 \sigma_{A,a}(p^0) \delta f^A_{p} \, , \end{split}\end{equation} where all the quantities without ``$\delta$'' are evaluated in equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-128: Now, let us note that, in global equilibrium, the Kirchhoff-Planck relation holds, namely $\kappa_A=\sigma_{A,a f^A_{p}$, and $f^A_{p}$ depends only on $p^0$.}
elements.sent-2411-129: Hence, \eqref{sgrom simplifies to \begin{equation}\label{asumptzio} \dfrac{1}{p^0} \delta [\mathcal{C}^A_{p}]_\text{``abs''} = -\sigma_{A,a}(p^0) \bigg(\dfrac{df^A_{p^0}}{dp^0} p^3 \delta u^3 +\delta f^A_p\bigg)\, ,\end{equation} which has indeed the form \eqref{griecz , with $\mathcal{M}\indices{^A _B}\propto \delta\indices{^A _B}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-130: If a scattering process (of a radiation particle against matter) conserves the energy of the scattered radiation, and is isotropic, the relative contribution to the Boltzmann collision integral takes the following form: \begin{equation}\begin{split} [\mathcal{C}^A_{p}]_{\text{``scat''}} ={} p^0 \sigma_{A,s}(p^0)\int_{\mathcal{S}^2} \dfrac{d^2\Omega'}{4\pi} (f^A_{p^0\mathbf{\Omega}'}-f^A_{p}) ={} \sigma_{A,s}(p^0) \int_{\mathcal{S}^2} \dfrac{d^2\Omega'}{4\pi} \int_0^{\infty} p'^0 dp'^0 \delta(p'^0{-} p^0) \, (f^A_{p'^0\mathbf{\Omega}'}-f^A_{p}) \, , \end{split}\end{equation} where $\sigma_{A,s}$ is the scattering coefficient, $d^2 \Omega$ is the solid angle element, and $\mathbf{\Omega}$ is a unit vector.
elements.sent-2411-131: Recalling that the volume element in spherical coordinates is $d^3p=(p'^0)^2 dp'^0 d^2\Omega$, we can rewrite the above integral as follows: \begin{equation}\label{in3d} [\mathcal{C}^A_{p}]_{\text{``scat''}} = \sigma_{A,s}(p^0)\int_{\mathbb{R}^3} \dfrac{d^3 p'}{4\pi p'^0} \delta(p'^0{-}p^0) (f^A_{p'} -f^A_{p} )\end{equation} Hence, recalling that $d^3p/p^0$ is the invariant measure in momentum space, equation \eqref{in3d corresponds to the following Lorentz-covariant expression, valid in all inertial frames: \begin{equation}\begin{split} [\mathcal{C}^A_{p}]_{\text{``scat''}} ={} \sigma_{A,s}(-u_\mu p^\mu) \int_{\mathbb{R}^3} \dfrac{d^3 p'}{4\pi p'^0} \delta(-u_\mu p'^\mu{+}u_\mu p^\mu) (f^A_{p'}-f^A_{p}) ={} \sigma_{A,s}(-u_\mu \Omega^\mu p^0) \int_{\mathbb{R}^3} \dfrac{d^3 p'}{4\pi p'^0} \delta(-u_\mu \Omega'^\mu p'^0{+}u_\mu \Omega^\mu p^0) (f^A_{p'}-f^A_{p}) ={} \sigma_{A,s}(-u_\mu \Omega^\mu p^0)\int_{\mathcal{S}^2} \dfrac{d^2\Omega'}{4\pi} \int_0^{\infty} \dfrac{p'^0 dp'^0}{-u_\mu \Omega'^\mu} \delta \bigg( p'^0{-}\dfrac{u_\mu \Omega^\mu}{u_\mu \Omega'^\mu} p^0 \bigg) (f^A_{p'^0\mathbf{\Omega}'}-f^A_{p}) ={} p^0 \sigma_{A,s}(-u_\mu \Omega^\mu p^0) \int_{\mathcal{S}^2} \dfrac{d^2\Omega'}{4\pi} \dfrac{ -u_\mu \Omega^\mu}{(-u_\mu \Omega'^\mu)^2} \bigg(f^A_{\frac{u_\mu \Omega^\mu}{u_\mu \Omega'^\mu} p^0\mathbf{\Omega}'}-f^A_{p} \bigg) \end{split}\end{equation} where we introduced the notation $\Omega^\mu=(1,\mathbf{\Omega )=p^\mu/p^0$.}}
elements.formula-2411-034: \begin{equation} [C^A_{p}]_{``scat''} ={} \sigma_{A,s}(-u_\mu p^\mu) \int_{R^3} \dfrac{d^3 p'}{4\pi p'^0} \delta(-u_\mu p'^\mu{+}u_\mu p^\mu) (f^A_{p'}-f^A_{p}) ={} \sigma_{A,s}(-u_\mu \Omega^\mu p^0) \int_{R^3} \dfrac{d^3 p'}{4\pi p'^0} \delta(-u_\mu \Omega'^\mu p'^0{+}u_\mu \Omega^\mu p^0) (f^A_{p'}-f^A_{p}) ={} \sigma_{A,s}(-u_\mu \Omega^\mu p^0)\int_{S^2} \dfrac{d^2\Omega'}{4\pi} \int_0^{\infty} \dfrac{p'^0 dp'^0}{-u_\mu \Omega'^\mu} \delta \bigg( p'^0{-}\dfrac{u_\mu \Omega^\mu}{u_\mu \Omega'^\mu} p^0 \bigg) (f^A_{p'^0\Omega'}-f^A_{p}) ={} p^0 \sigma_{A,s}(-u_\mu \Omega^\mu p^0) \int_{S^2} \dfrac{d^2\Omega'}{4\pi} \dfrac{ -u_\mu \Omega^\mu}{(-u_\mu \Omega'^\mu)^2} \bigg(f^A_{\frac{u_\mu \Omega^\mu}{u_\mu \Omega'^\mu} p^0\Omega'}-f^A_{p} \bigg) \end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-228: In this section, we prove that current simulations with M1 closure do not include an actual radiative shear viscosity.
elements.sent-2411-229: To avoid possible misunderstandings, let us state precisely what we mean by that.
elements.sent-2411-230: To have ``shear viscosity'', a hydrodynamic theory does not need to have an explicit gradient correction to the stress tensor, proportional to $\partial_{1 u_{3}$.}
elements.sent-2411-231: Any form of shear momentum flux $T_{13}$ may play the role of an effective shear viscosity, if it causes friction between two layers in relative motion.
elements.sent-2411-232: However, for this flux to act as a genuine shear viscosity, we need a mechanism that will ``turn on'' this shear stress in the presence of any shear flow.
elements.sent-2411-233: In other words, we need some term in the equation of motion (of whatever form) that ``activates'' $T_{13}$ when the flow has a geometry as in figure 1 of the main text.
elements.sent-2411-234: Here, we show that the M1 closure does not have such a term.
elements.sent-2411-235: In particular, we prove that there exist exact stationary solutions of M1 radiation hydrodynamics where the fluid layers are (and forever will be) experiencing shear motion, and they keep sliding against each other with no friction.
elements.sent-2411-236: Thus, we have that \begin{equation}\delta \varepsilon_R = \sum_A g_A \int \dfrac{d^3 p}{(2\pi)^3} p^0 \delta f^A_p=0 \, , \spc \spc \delta F^1 = \sum_A g_A \int \dfrac{d^3 p}{(2\pi)^3} p^1 \delta f^A_p=0 \, .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-242: For completeness, let us also discuss the ``multi-frequency'' method.
elements.sent-2411-243: The latter is a refinement of M1 radiation hydrodynamics, where one does not just track the total radiation energy density and flux.
elements.sent-2411-244: Instead, one separately evolves a large number of radiation energy densities and fluxes, each counting photons (or neutrinos) within a small frequency window.

elements.section-2411-004: Decay of a vortex by radiative friction
elements.sent-2411-263: In this section, we give an example of a two-dimensional solenoidal (i.e. incompressible) flow that decays due to radiative friction.
elements.sent-2411-264: This can be expressed as a Fourier superposition of transversal plane waves, meaning that our results still apply (with the same dispersion relations), and we can use our model to describe the full evolution.
elements.sent-2411-265: We consider the vector potential \begin{equation}\mathbb{A}= \dfrac{vL}{2} e^{-(x^2+y^2)/L^2} \begin{pmatrix} 0 0 1 \end{pmatrix}\end{equation} where $v$ and $L$ are some positive constants.
elements.sent-2411-266: This generates the incompressible flow \begin{equation}\label{iniziovortex} u=\nabla \times \mathbb{A}=\dfrac{v}{L} e^{-\frac{x^2+y^2}{L^2}} \begin{pmatrix} -y x 0 \end{pmatrix}\, ,\end{equation} which has the shape of a localized cylindrically symmetric vortex (see figure \ref{fig:NavierVoertex , left panel).}
elements.sent-2411-267: Its radius is approximately $L$, which defines the hydrodynamic lengthscale.
elements.sent-2411-268:
Taking this shape as our initial condition, we can solve the linearised incompressible Navier-Stokes equation analytically, and we obtain (defining the kinematic viscosity $
u =\eta^{(1) /W$)  egin{equation}\label{navotex} u(t,x,y)=vL^3 \, \dfrac{e^{- rac{x^2+y^2}{L^2+4
u t}}}{(L^2+4
u t)^2}  egin{pmatrix} -y x 0 \end{pmatrix}\, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-269: As can be seen from figure \ref{fig:NavierVoertex (right panel), the vortex decays by diffusing its angular momentum to the outer layers.}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.48\linewidth]{Vortex.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.49\linewidth]{NavierVortex.pdf} \caption{Let panel: Initial state of our vortex, as given by equation \eqref{iniziovortex}. The motion is perfectly incompressible and circular. The tangential speed is maximal at $r=L/\sqrt{2}$, where $r=\sqrt{x^2{+}y^2}$ is the distance from the axis of rotation. Right panel: Tangential speed as a function of $r$ along the Navier-Stokes solution \eqref{navotex}. The dashed line is the initial profile. The orange line is the shape at $\nu t=0.3$. The location of the maximum moves outwards, being located at $r=\sqrt{(L^2{+}4\nu t)/2}$.} \label{fig:NavierVoertex}
elements.sent-2411-270: Let us now compute the non-Newtonian corrections due to the finite photon mean free path.
elements.sent-2411-271: To this end, we first note that the initial shape \eqref{iniziovortex can be expressed as the following Fourier integral: \begin{equation}\mathbf{u}(0,x,y) = \dfrac{iv L^3}{8\pi} \int dk_x dk_y \, e^{-\frac{L^2}{4}(k_x^2 +k_y^2)+i(k_x x+k_y y)} \begin{pmatrix} k_y -k_x 0 \end{pmatrix}\, .\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-084: \begin{equation}u(0,x,y) = \dfrac{iv L^3}{8\pi} \int dk_x dk_y \, e^{-\frac{L^2}{4}(k_x^2 +k_y^2)+i(k_x x+k_y y)}  k_y -k_x 0 \, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-272: Each plane wave contributing to this integral is transversal, namely $\mathbf{k}\cdot \mathbf{u (\mathbf{k )=0$, which follows from the vector field being solenoidal.}}
elements.sent-2411-273: Hence, all these waves evolve according to the equations shown in the main text, up to a rotation in space.
elements.sent-2411-274: In particular, they all decay with the dispersion relation $\omega(\sqrt{k_x^2 +k_y^2 )$ computed in the main text, giving \begin{equation}\mathbf{u}(t,x,y) = \dfrac{iv L^3}{8\pi} \int dk_x dk_y \, e^{-\frac{L^2}{4}(k_x^2 +k_y^2)+i(k_x x+k_y y)-i\omega(\sqrt{k_x^2+k_y^2})t} \begin{pmatrix} k_y -k_x 0 \end{pmatrix}\, .\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-085: \begin{equation}u(t,x,y) = \dfrac{iv L^3}{8\pi} \int dk_x dk_y \, e^{-\frac{L^2}{4}(k_x^2 +k_y^2)+i(k_x x+k_y y)-i\omega(\sqrt{k_x^2+k_y^2})t}  k_y -k_x 0 \, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-275: To determine the tangential speed of the vortex as a function of the distance, we can just set $y=0$ and $x=r$.
elements.sent-2411-276: Then, $ u^x$ vanishes, and we have that $ u=| \mathbf{u |= u^y$.}
elements.sent-2411-277: Working in polar coordinates, $\{k_x=k\cos \theta,k_y=k\sin \theta\}$, we can integrate out the angle, and we finally obtain the expression below, which can be used to compare different approaches (see figure \ref{fig:nonewtvortex ): \begin{equation}\label{utr} u(t,r) = \dfrac{v L^3}{4} \int_0^{+\infty} k^2 J_1(kr) e^{-\frac{L^2k^2}{4}-i\omega(k)t} dk \, .\end{equation}.}

Each plot shows the initial state (dashed), the solution of the Boltzmann equation (blue), the non-Newtonian model provided in the main text (red), and the Navier-Stokes solution (orange). The quantity $\tau/L$ is the radiative Knudsen number, and it quantifies the size of the non-Newtonian corrections. For small $\tau/L$, all curves overlap. For $\tau/L=1$, the non-Newtonian model fares better than Navier-Stokes, but it is not perfect.
